segments.purgatorio-10-g1: Poi (then) begins the narrative sequence. Fummo is the passato remoto (or archaic perfect) of essere used here to mean 'we went/were'—with dentro al soglio de la porta indicating location: 'inside the threshold of the gate.' Che introduces a relative clause modifying porta. ’l mal amor de l’anime is a noun phrase 'the evil love of souls'; disusa here functions like an adjectival/predicative past participle (or archaic verbal form) meaning 'put away / made disused' — the gate is said to 'disuse' or put an end to that evil love.
segments.purgatorio-10-g2: Perché introduces a causal clause 'because.' Fa parer is a causative construction: fare + infinitive (parere) = 'cause to seem' or 'makes appear.' Dritta is a predicative adjective 'straight' and la via torta is the noun phrase 'the crooked road' that is being made to seem straight.
segments.purgatorio-10-g3: Sonando is a present participle (absolute or circumstantial) meaning 'sounding' or 'as it sounded.' La senti' is an archaic/poetic first-person form of sentire (I heard/felt). Esser is the infinitive of essere used with the past participle richiusa to form a passive/state 'to be shut (again)'. The whole means roughly 'as it sounded, I perceived it to be shut again.'
segments.purgatorio-10-g4: E is simply 'and.' S’io is the contraction of se io (if I); avesse is the imperfect subjunctive (or conditional-in-subordinate) of avere used in a counterfactual/hypothetical: 'if I had.' Li occhi vòlti is a participial phrase where vòlti (from volgere) means 'turned' — 'my eyes turned toward it.' Ad essa = 'to/at it' (the gate).
segments.purgatorio-10-g5: Qual = quale 'what / what kind of.' Fora stata is a conditional/periphrastic form (fora = conditional of essere) + stata (past participle): 'would have been.' Al fallo = 'for the fault' (i.e. in excuse for the fault/sin). Degna scusa = 'a fitting/worthy excuse.' The line asks rhetorically what excuse would have been adequate.
segments.purgatorio-10-g6: Noi is the subject 'we.' Salavam is an archaic/local tense of the verb (contextually 'we went/stepped/ascended' or 'we crossed over') with per una pietra fessa indicating the means or path: 'over a split/cleft stone.' Pietra fessa is a compound: 'pietra' (stone) + 'fessa' (split, cleft).
segments.purgatorio-10-g7: Che introduces a relative clause modifying pietra fessa: 'which moved.' Si moveva is the reflexive/impersonal verb 'moved itself' (i.e. it moved). E d’una e d’altra parte is an idiomatic expression 'on one side and on the other' — 'to and fro' or 'both this way and that.'
segments.purgatorio-10-g8: Sì come = 'just as / like.' L’onda che fugge e s’appressa is a simile: 'the wave that flees and draws near.' Fugge is present/factual 'flees/retreats'; s’appressa is the reflexive form meaning 'approaches' or 'comes near.'
segments.purgatorio-10-g9: Qui 'here.' Si conviene is an impersonal construction meaning 'it is fitting / one ought' (conviene + infinitive). Usare is the infinitive 'to use'; un poco d'arte = 'a little skill/art/cunning' (arte here means skill or technique). The phrase is an instruction: 'Here one should use a little skill.'
segments.purgatorio-10-g10: Cominciò is the past tense 'began' (3rd singular). ’l duca mio = 'my duke' (Dante's guide, Virgil): the definite article l' + duca, plus the possessive mio.
segments.purgatorio-10-g11: In introduces the infinitive reflexive accostarsi 'to approach / to draw near' (here used with directional adverbs that follow). Or (now) quinci means 'on this side / from here' — 'accostarsi or quinci' = 'approach now from this side.'
segments.purgatorio-10-g12: Or quindi = 'now from there / on that side.' Al lato = 'to the side / toward the flank.' Che introduces a relative clause modifying lato; si parte (from partire) here means 'it parts / it moves away' — 'the side toward which it moves off.' The whole clause instructs alternating approaches toward different sides.
segments.purgatorio-10-g13: E = 'and.' Questo refers back to the preceding action (the careful stepping/skill) as the cause. Fece is 'made' and i nostri passi scarsi = 'our steps scarce' = 'our steps few' or 'we took few steps' (i nostri passi is the subject of scarsi, which qualifies them as scanty).
segments.purgatorio-10-g14: Tanto che introduces a result or temporal clause 'so that / to such an extent that.' Pria = 'before.' Lo scemo de la luna uses scemo (from scemare) as a substantive meaning 'the waning of the moon' (i.e. the moon's decrease). Rigiunse is a past tense 'rejoined / reached again' and al letto suo 'to her bed' (horizon or resting-place); per ricorcarsi is an infinitive/reflexive verb 'to repeat her circuit / to return/turn again' — an archaic poetic verb describing the moon's motion.
segments.purgatorio-10-g15: Che introduces the clause giving the result/time: noi fossimo fuor di quella cruna. Fossimo is the imperfect subjunctive of essere used in a temporal/result clause after tanta/pria — 'we were (came to be) outside' — fuor di = 'out of/ outside' and quella cruna = 'that eye/narrow opening' (cruna = small hole or figuratively narrow pass).
segments.purgatorio-10-g16: Ma quando = 'but when.' Fummo (past of essere) 'we were' liberi e aperti = 'free and open/exposed' (i.e. no longer constrained). Sù dove = 'up where'; il monte in dietro si rauna = 'the mountain behind gathers itself' (rauna from raunare 'to gather, collect' — Dante uses it of the mountain forming/collecting behind them). The clause locates them on the mountain where it rounds/gathers backward.
segments.purgatorio-10-g17: ïo is a dialectal/poetic form of io 'I.' Stancato is a past participle used adjectivally 'weary/tired.' E amendue means 'and both (of us),' with amendue = 'both' (archaic). Incerti = 'uncertain' and di nostra via = 'as to our way/path' — the speaker and his companion are both unsure which way to go.
segments.purgatorio-10-g18: Restammo is the passato remoto of restare 'we remained / we halted.' In su = 'on / upon' (here 'on a plain'). Un piano solingo = 'a solitary/plain plain' (solingo = solitary, archaic). Più che strade per diserti is a comparative phrase: 'more (solitary) than roads through deserts' — 'per' here = 'across / through' and diserti = 'deserted/desert places' (i.e. lonelier than desert tracks).
segments.purgatorio-10-g109: Li is the archaic masculine plural definite article (modern Italian gli), here introducing the subject. Occhi is the plural noun 'eyes' and miei is the possessive 'my' placed after the noun (a common poetic order) and agreeing in gender/number with occhi.
segments.purgatorio-10-g110: Ch’ is the relative pronoun 'which/that' introducing a clause modifying 'occhi'. A + infinitive (a mirare) is an older Italian construction meaning 'to (be) gazing' or 'for gazing'; eran is the imperfect tense 'were' and contenti is a predicative adjective 'satisfied/ content' agreeing with occhi. Per veder expresses purpose 'in order to see' and novitadi is the poetic plural 'novelties' or 'new things'. Ond' (contraction of onde) = 'from/for which'; e’ here is an archaic/clitic form referring back to the novelties (literally 'they' or 'to them' in older usage), and son is 'are'; vaghi in this context means 'eager/desirous' (an archaic sense of vago), so the clause says the eyes were glad to gaze to see novelties for which they are eager.
segments.purgatorio-10-g111: Volgendosi is the reflexive gerund/participle 'turning themselves' (the reflexive si shows the eyes as turning). Ver' is the poetic contraction of verso, meaning 'toward' (an archaic form); lui is the indirect object 'him'. Non furon is the past (passato remoto/imperfect literary form in Dante) 'were not' and lenti is the adjective 'slow' — together 'they were not slow (to turn)'. The subject carried through the clause is 'the eyes'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g112: Non precedes the verb to negate the clause. vo' is an archaic contracted form of voglio (I want / I wish); però is an adverb here meaning “however.” Together Non vo' però expresses “I do not (want), however.”
segments.purgatorio-10-g113: Lettor is a vocative address to the reader; its final -e is dropped as an archaic/poetic form (lettor = lettore). It stands outside the syntax of the sentence as direct address.
segments.purgatorio-10-g114: Che introduces a subordinate clause functioning as the object of vo' (Non voglio che ...). Tu is the subject; ti is the reflexive pronoun required by the verb smaghi. Smaghi is the second-person singular present subjunctive (archaic/poetic morphology) used after a verb of volition (non voglio che ...) to express what the speaker does not want. Di marks the relationship (about/regarding) with buon proponimento (“good resolution/intention”). Per + infinitive (per udire) gives purpose/reason (“because of, on hearing, for hearing”). Come introduces an indirect interrogative/clausedegree ‘how’ or ‘the way’ God wills; vuol is present indicative of volere (God wills/wants). The second che introduces another subordinate clause requiring the subjunctive (che ’l debito si paghi): ’l is the archaic contraction of il (the), si is the reflexive/passive clitic used to form a passive/impersonal (here passive: “be paid”), and paghi is third-person singular present subjunctive required by vuol che.
segments.purgatorio-10-g115: Non negates the imperative/admonition. Attender here means “attend to / give heed to” (not merely ‘wait for’); the verb is used in the infinitive after the negative imperative sense. La forma = the outward shape/appearance (forma used concretely). Del is the contraction of di + il (of the). Martìre is an older spelling; here it refers to the suffering/martyrdom (the state or role of the sufferer).
segments.purgatorio-10-g116: Pensa is the (imperative/hortative) second-person singular or an exhortatory present “think/consider.” La succession means “the succession/sequence”; the poet calls the reader to consider the order of events rather than the outward form.
segments.purgatorio-10-g117: Pensa again issues the exhortation “think/consider.” Ch’ (= che) introduces the subordinate clause “that ...”. Al is the contraction a + il (“to/at the” but here part of the expression ‘to the worse’ meaning ‘toward worse’). Peggio means “worse.” Oltre is a preposition/adverb meaning “beyond” or “further than.” La gran sentenza = “the great sentence/judgment” (gran is an aphetic/generic form of grande used for metrical reasons). Non può ire uses può (present indicative of potere, “is able / can”) plus the infinitive ire (“go”), an idiom meaning “can go” or “can come to,” so the clause says it cannot go on to anything worse.
segments.purgatorio-10-g118: Io is the stressed subject pronoun 'I' (optional in Italian but used here for emphasis). Cominciai is the first-person singular passato remoto (past simple) of cominciare, 'I began'; the colon marks the start of direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-10-g119: Maestro is a vocative: Dante addresses his guide directly. Here it functions as a form of address meaning 'Master' (Virgil as his teacher/guide).
segments.purgatorio-10-g120: This whole chunk is a relative clause: quel = the neuter demonstrative 'that/that which'; ch' is the elided form of che (relative 'which/that'); io veggio is present first-person 'I see' (veggio is Dante's form of 'vedo'); muovere is the infinitive meaning 'to move' and completes the perception verb (I see them moving); a noi = 'toward us' with noi used as the object of the preposition (so 'moving toward us').
segments.purgatorio-10-g121: Non is simple negation. Mi is the indirect-object clitic 'to me' (giving the experiencer of the perception). Sembian is an archaic/poetic third-person plural present form of sembiare/sembrar (modern sembrano) 'they seem'; it agrees with the idea of multiple visible beings. Persone is the predicate noun 'people/persons'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g122: E = 'and', linking clauses. Non so = 'I do not know' (so is first-person singular of sapere). Che here functions as an interrogative/indefinite 'what' (short for 'what they are' or 'what it is').
segments.purgatorio-10-g123: Sì is an adverb 'so/so much' (degree adverb). Nel is the contraction of in + il and with an infinitive (nel veder) means 'in seeing' or 'when I see'. Vaneggio is used as 'I rave/am delirious' (first-person sense in context).
segments.purgatorio-10-g19: 'Da' is the preposition 'from'; here it begins a locative phrase. 'La sua sponda' = 'its bank/shore' (article + possessive before the noun, standard word order). Note the poem keeps 'da la' separate rather than the usual contracted 'dalla'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g20: 'ove' is a literary form of 'where' introducing a relative/location clause. 'confina' is 3rd-person singular present 'borders/joins on'; 'il vano' uses 'vano' as a substantive meaning 'empty space, hollow' rather than the adjective 'vain'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g21: 'al' (a + il) = 'to the/at the'; 'piè' (poetic spelling of 'piede') = 'foot/base'; 'de' is archaic for 'di' ('of'); 'l'alta ripa' = 'the high bank/cliff'; the relative clause 'che pur sale' uses 'pur' = 'still' and 'sale' (3rd sg) = 'rises/ascends', so 'that still rises'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g22: 'misurrebbe' is an archaic conditional/interpretation of 'misurare' (literary form from 'misurre') meaning 'would measure'. 'in tre volte' = 'in three (times/units)' — i.e. three times the length of — and the logical object is 'un corpo umano' ('a human body').
segments.purgatorio-10-g23: 'e quanto' = 'and as much/as far as'; 'l'occhio mio' = 'my eye' (article + noun + possessive); 'potea' is the imperfect 'could/was able' (ongoing past ability); 'trar' is the apocopated infinitive of 'trarre' ('to draw/extend'), a poetical shortening; 'd'ale' = 'di ale' literally 'of wings' (a metaphorical measure: 'as far as wing-lengths').
segments.purgatorio-10-g24: The paired 'or ... or ...' (here 'or' = 'now') shows alternation: 'now from the left and now from the right flank.' 'dal' = 'da + il' = 'from the'; 'sinistro'/'destro' = 'left'/'right'; 'fianco' = 'flank/side'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g25: 'Questa cornice' = 'this cornice/ledge'; 'mi parea' = 'seemed to me' where 'mi' is the indirect object 'to me' and 'parea' is the imperfect of 'parere' (literary spelling 'parea' = 'pareva'); 'cotale' = 'such'/'of such a sort/size' referring back to the measured extent.
segments.purgatorio-10-g26: 'Là sù' = 'up there' (two adverbs); 'non eran mossi' is an older/periphrastic past: 'eran' (imperfect of 'essere') + past participle 'mossi' = 'had been moved' / 'were not yet moved' (meaning 'we had not yet set our feet in motion'); 'i piè nostri' = 'our feet'; 'anco' = 'yet/still' (here negative + 'anco' = 'not yet').
segments.purgatorio-10-g27: 'quand' io conobbi' = 'when I recognized' (quand' is elided 'quando'); 'conobbi' is the passato remoto of 'conoscere' (archaic/spoken past = 'I recognized'); 'quella ripa intorno' = 'that bank/ledge around'; the relative clause 'che dritto di salita aveva manco' literally 'that had less of a straight ascent' — 'aveva manco' = 'lacked' or 'was less endowed with', so the bank's ascent was not as straight/steep.
segments.purgatorio-10-g28: 'esser' is the infinitive 'to be' used in reported description (dependent on 'conobbi ... quella ripa ... esser ...'); 'di marmo candido' = 'of white marble'; 'e addorno d'intagli sì' = 'and adorned with (so many/such) carvings' — 'addorno' is participial/adjectival, and 'd'intagli' = 'of carvings'; 'sì' intensifies and links to the following clause.
segments.purgatorio-10-g29: 'che non pur Policleto,' begins a consequence/comparison clause: 'so (ornamented) that not only Polyclitus ...' Here 'non pur' = 'not only' (lit. 'not even/even not'); 'Policleto' names the famed classical sculptor used as a standard of excellence.
segments.purgatorio-10-g30: 'ma la natura lì avrebbe scorno.' = 'but Nature there would have shame' i.e. 'even Nature would be ashamed there.' 'avrebbe' is the conditional of 'avere' ('would have') used idiomatically with 'scorno' ('shame, disgrace') to mean 'would feel ashamed.'
segments.purgatorio-10-g31: 'L'angel che venne in terra' = 'the angel who came to earth' ('venne' = past historic of 'venire'). 'col decreto' = 'with the decree' (col = con + il). 'de la molt' anni lagrimata pace' is a compact poetic phrase: 'de' = 'of', 'molt'' = elided 'molti'/'molta' meaning 'many'/'long', 'anni' = 'years', 'lagrimata' = 'wept/long-lamented' (past participle modifying 'pace'), so 'the decree of the peace long lamented for many years.'
segments.purgatorio-10-g32: 'ch’aperse' = 'that opened' (ch' = che); 'il ciel' = 'the sky/heaven'; 'del suo lungo divieto' = 'from/of its long prohibition/interdiction' — 'del' = di + il and 'suo' is possessive; the clause explains the angel's action, opening heaven after a long ban.
segments.purgatorio-10-g33: 'dinanzi a noi' = 'before us/in front of us'; 'pareva' (imperfect of 'parere') = 'seemed'; 'sì verace' = 'so true/lifelike'; 'quivi intagliato' = 'carved there' (quivi = there; 'intagliato' past participle used adjectivally); 'in un atto soave' = 'in a gentle/soft gesture/pose' (atto = act/gesture).
segments.purgatorio-10-g34: 'che non sembiava imagine che tace.' — 'che non sembiava' = 'that it did not seem'; 'imagine' = 'an image/likeness'; the final relative clause 'che tace' = 'that is silent' (tace = present 'is silent/mute'), so the whole line says the carving looked so alive it did not seem to be a silent image.
segments.purgatorio-10-g35: 'Giurato si saria' is an impersonal construction: 'one would have sworn' (Giurato = past participle 'sworn' used impersonally; 'si saria' = archaic conditional of 'essere'/'saria' = 'would be' — together giving 'one would have sworn'). 'ch'el dicesse' ('that he would say') uses 'dicesse' (imperfect subjunctive) to report the imagined utterance; 'Ave!' is the exclamation 'Hail!'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g36: This segment is a punctuation mark (an apostrophe and semicolon) closing the preceding utterance; it carries no lexical material to analyze and serves only to mark the end of speech/thought.
segments.purgatorio-10-g37: "perché" here functions as a causal conjunction meaning 'for' or 'because' introducing the reason. "iv' era imaginata quella" uses an archaic location adverb (iv' = ivi = 'there') plus imperfect 'era' (was) and a past participle used adjectivally ('imaginata' = 'depicted/represented'), so literally 'there was represented that one.' The relative clause "ch’ad aprir l’alto amor volse la chiave" contains the contracted relative "ch'" = che (who/which) and an infinitive with the marker "ad" (ad aprir = 'to open'); "volse" is the literary preterite of volgere meaning 'turned' (i.e. 'turned the key'), and "l'alto amor" is a poetic phrase 'the lofty/loftiest Love' (personified).
segments.purgatorio-10-g38: The conjunction "e" links this clause to the previous. "avea" is the imperfect of avere used here in the sense 'had' (possession/held) and introduces a verbal phrase describing a posture/gesture: "in atto impressa" means 'impressed/printed in (her) gesture/pose.' "esta favella" = 'this speech/phrase' (favella = words or saying) and the Latin phrase "Ecce ancilla Deï" is cited directly (hence left untranslated in the text).
segments.purgatorio-10-g39: "propriamente" is an adverb meaning 'properly' or 'exactly' qualifying the simile that follows. "come figura in cera si suggella" is a simile: "come" = 'as/just as', "figura in cera" = 'a figure in wax', and "si suggella" uses the pronominal/passive se (impersonal/passive si) plus the verb 'suggellare' = 'to seal' — literally 'is sealed' (i.e. the phrase is impressed as a wax seal is impressed on a wax figure).
segments.purgatorio-10-g40: "Non tener pur ad un loco la mente" is an imperative prohibition. "Non tener" = 'do not keep/hold'; "pur" here means 'only/merely' or 'solely'; "ad un loco" = 'in/at one place'; the direct object "la mente" ('the mind') follows the prepositional phrase — typical word order in Dante is flexible, but the negative imperative + infinitive construction is straightforward: 'do not keep the mind solely in one place.'
segments.purgatorio-10-g41: "disse 'l dolce maestro" is simple past narrative: "disse" = 'said', the article contraction "'l" = 'il' precedes the epithet "dolce maestro" = 'sweet/dear master' (Virgil). This is a standard subject-verb phrase in reported speech.
segments.purgatorio-10-g42: The relative clause "che m'avea da quella parte onde 'l cuore ha la gente" modifies "maestro." "che" = 'who/which'; "m'avea" = 'had me' (imperfect of avere with clitic object 'mi'); "da quella parte" = 'on/from that side'; "onde" = 'from/whence' introducing the final relative/locative clause; "'l cuore ha la gente" is elliptical: literally 'where the heart has the people' meaning 'the side where the heart is in people' (i.e. the left side). Word order shows Dante's use of clitics and relative connectors—"m'avea" places the clitic before the verb in an imperfect narrative form.
segments.purgatorio-10-g43: "Per ch'i' mi mossi col viso" is a causal/resultive phrase: "Per" here (with the following "ch'" = che/because) gives the reason or consequence 'whereupon/therefore'; "i'" = io (I); "mi mossi" is the first-person preterite (mossi) of muovere with clitic 'mi' = 'I moved myself' i.e. 'I turned'; "col viso" = 'with my face' (meaning 'I turned my face'). Note the archaic contractions (Per ch' = perché/wherefore) and the enclitic placement of pronouns.
segments.purgatorio-10-g44: "e vedea di retro da Maria" continues the narrative: "e vedea" = 'and I saw' (imperfect 'vedea' = saw/was seeing); "di retro da Maria" = 'behind Mary' (literally 'from behind Mary' — "di retro" = behind, "da" marks the reference point). The imperfect tense keeps the visual scene contemporaneous with the narrator's movement.
segments.purgatorio-10-g45: "da quella costa onde m'era colui che mi movea" is a complex locative relative: "da quella costa" = 'from that slope/side' (costa = side/slope here); "onde" = 'from which/whence' introduces the subordinate clause; "m'era colui che mi movea" literally 'there was he who moved me' (imperfect 'era' and 'movea'), i.e. 'from which side he who moved me was' — Dante places clitics and verbs as typical of older Italian syntax.
segments.purgatorio-10-g46: "un'altra storia ne la roccia imposta" describes the object seen: "un'altra storia" = 'another story/scene' (storia = scene/representation here); "ne la roccia" = 'in/into the rock' (ne = in); "imposta" is a past participle from imporre used adjectivally meaning 'set into/placed' (i.e. 'set into the rock'). The phrase is a concise pictorial description: another tableau carved into the rock.
segments.purgatorio-10-g47: "per ch'io varcai Virgilio" again uses "per ch'" (wherefore/so) to introduce the action: "io varcai" is the first-person preterite of varcare ('I crossed/passed by'); the object/name "Virgilio" follows. The clause records Dante's action of passing by Virgil to inspect the scene.
segments.purgatorio-10-g48: "e fe'mi presso" is concisely phrased and uses an archaic contraction: the sense is 'and made me draw near' or 'and brought me near.' The clitic "mi" is the object of the (ellipsed or contracted) verb that means 'to make/bring' (fe' standing for fece or a related contraction), and "presso" = 'near.' The construction shows Dante's fondness for clitic clusters and contracted forms.
segments.purgatorio-10-g49: "acciò che fosse a li occhi miei disposta" is a purpose/result clause: "acciò che" = 'so that' or 'in order that', followed by the subjunctive "fosse" (imperfect subjunctive) meaning 'it might be' or 'so that it was/should be'; "a li occhi miei" = 'to my eyes' (i.e. before my eyes); "disposta" = 'arranged/placed' (past participle used adjectivally). The subjunctive signals purpose or intention: he brought Dante near so that the scene would be placed before his eyes.
segments.purgatorio-10-g50: "Era intagliato lì nel marmo stesso lo carro e ' buoi" is a passive/stative description: "Era intagliato" = 'was carved/was engraved', "lì" = 'there', "nel marmo stesso" = 'in the very marble' (stesso = 'very/same'); the nominative phrase "lo carro e ' buoi" ('the cart and the oxen') is the thing carved. Dante often uses the imperfect/passive to describe static images set in the landscape.
segments.purgatorio-10-g51: "traendo l'arca santa" is a participial phrase modifying the cart and oxen: "traendo" (present participle) = 'drawing' or 'leading' (the ark), "l'arca santa" = 'the holy Ark.' The participle expresses simultaneous action in the carved scene: the cart and oxen are depicted as drawing the Ark.
segments.purgatorio-10-g52: "per che si teme officio non commesso" is a causal/result clause: "per che" = 'whereby/for which' introduces the consequence or the interpretation of the image; "si teme" uses the impersonal/passive "si" + teme (it is feared / one fears); "officio non commesso" = 'a duty not committed/fulfilled' (commesso here as past participle of commettere). The whole line means 'from which it is feared that a duty was left unfulfilled.'
segments.purgatorio-10-g88: A single punctuation mark (semicolon) separating the preceding speech from what follows.
segments.purgatorio-10-g89: ed = e with vowel elision used as the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking to the next speaker; ei is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'he' (the speaker).
segments.purgatorio-10-g90: Chi = 'whoever' (indefinite relative); fia is the future/subjunctive form of essere here meaning 'will be'; dov' is the contracted form of dove = 'where'; io = 'I' — together: 'whoever will be where I am'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g91: la is the feminine direct object pronoun 'it' (referring back to 'vengeance' or 'that'); ti = 'to you'; farà is future of fare = 'will do' or 'will make' — 'will do it for you'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g92: ed = 'and' again linking the next speaker; ella = 'she' (the woman who replies).
segments.purgatorio-10-g93: L’ (elided article) + altrui bene = 'the good of another' or 'another's good' (altrui = 'of others/another's'); a te = 'to you'; che = 'what/that' introducing the question clause; fia = 'will be' — literally: 'What good that belongs to another will be to you...'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g94: se = 'if' introducing the conditional; ’l (il) + tuo = 'your' (literally 'the yours'); metti = 2nd-person present 'you put' (here 'you set/put'); in oblio = 'into oblivion/forgetfulness' — 'if you put your own into oblivion'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g95: A semicolon marking the end of the previous quoted speech and the start of the reply.
segments.purgatorio-10-g96: ond' (from 'onde') here = 'whereupon'/'therefore' introducing the reaction; elli is an archaic form for 'he' (the speaker).
segments.purgatorio-10-g97: Or = 'now'; ti = 'you' (object); conforta is the imperative or present meaning 'comfort'/'take heart' — 'Now take heart'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g98: ch’ = contraction of che = 'for/that'; ei = 'it/he' (impersonal subject here: 'it is fitting'); convene (convene) = 'is fitting'/'is necessary'; ch’ i = 'that I' (che io) with solva = present subjunctive of solvere meaning 'pay/fulfill/discharge'; il mio dovere = 'my duty'; anzi = 'before' in the temporal sense here; ch’ i mova = 'that I move/set out' (subjunctive) — overall: 'for it is fitting that I discharge my duty before I set out.'
segments.purgatorio-10-g99: giustizia = 'justice' (here the demand of right/divine justice); vuole = 'requires/demands' (present of volere); e = 'and'; pietà = 'pity'/'mercy'; mi = 'me'; ritene = 'holds back'/'restrains' (present) — 'justice requires it, and pity restrains me.'
segments.purgatorio-10-g100: Colui che mai non vide cosa nova is a relative-subject phrase: Colui (demonstrative pronoun, 'he who') + che (relative 'who') introduce the relative clause. Mai non vide is the passato remoto verb phrase ('never saw') with mai + non giving the negative 'never'. Cosa nova is the object ('anything new'); the main verb of the whole clause is produsse (passato remoto 'produced'), and esto visibile parlare functions as the direct object where parlare (an infinitive used substantively) means 'speech/utterance' and esto (archaic 'this') and visibile qualify it.
segments.purgatorio-10-g101: Novello a noi is a short predicate meaning 'new to us' (novello = 'new'; a noi = 'to us'). Perché here introduces the reason ('because'), and qui non si trova is an impersonal/reflexive construction where si trova (present tense of trovare) means 'is found' — with non it means 'is not found here.'
segments.purgatorio-10-g102: Mentr' (contraction of mentre) + io mi dilettava is an imperfect indicative ('while I was taking delight') with the reflexive mi marking the experiencer. Di guardare is the verb's governed infinitive 'in looking at.' L' imagini (elided article + plural) di tante umilitadi is a genitive phrase 'the images of so many humilities,' with tante = 'so many.'
segments.purgatorio-10-g103: E per lo fabbro loro uses per in the causal/benefactive sense 'because of/for' and lo fabbro loro = 'their Maker' (archaic lo + fabbro + possessive loro). A veder care is an idiomatic infinitive phrase 'to be dear to see' (a veder + adjective care 'dear/precious'), so the clause means they were pleasing/precious to behold because of their Maker.
segments.purgatorio-10-g104: Ecco is an interjection meaning 'behold' or 'look'; di qua is a locative expression 'on this side / over here' (di = 'from/of', qua = 'here'). The phrase calls attention to something in the scene.
segments.purgatorio-10-g105: Ma fanno i passi radi is a short clause: ma = 'but'; fanno (present of fare) = 'they take/make' and i passi radi = 'the steps are sparse/infrequent.' In context it idiomatically means 'they move with short/infrequent steps' (they advance slowly).
segments.purgatorio-10-g106: Mormorava is imperfect tense 'he was murmuring' (ongoing past action), and il poeta is the subject 'the poet.' The imperfect frames this as a low, continuing remark made while the speaker watched.
segments.purgatorio-10-g107: Molte genti is simply 'many people' (molte = 'many', genti = 'people'). The phrase is an exclamation or the start of the poet's comment identifying the crowd.
segments.purgatorio-10-g108: Questi ne ’nvïeranno a li alti gradi is future tense: questi ('these' / 'those people') is subject; ne is the partitive pronoun 'of them / some (of them)'; ’nvïeranno (inv ieranno) is future 'will send'; a li alti gradi = 'to the high steps/degrees.' The diaeresis in nvïeranno marks vowel separation in the old orthography.
segments.purgatorio-10-g140: Come introduces a simile here meaning 'just as' or 'as'. Per + infinitive (per sostentar) expresses purpose/role 'to support'; sostentar is an older infinitive form of sostenere/sostentare and means 'to support'. Solaio o tetto are the two objects being supported ('a floor/ceiling or a roof').
segments.purgatorio-10-g141: Per mensola here means 'on/by means of a corbel or bracket' (per = by means of); talvolta = 'sometimes'. Una figura is the subject; si vede is the impersonal/passive 'one sees' or 'is seen'. Giugner (archaic infinitive giugnere/giungere) means 'to come/bring (to a point)', so giugner le ginocchia al petto = 'bring the knees to the chest'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g142: La qual is a relative phrase 'which' referring back to the figure; fa = 'makes/causes'. Del = di + il 'of/from the'. Non ver vera is a compressed poetic phrase: 'non ver' = 'not-true' (something unreal), vera emphasizes 'real' or 'true' bitterness; rancura = 'bitterness/resentment'. Nascere here means 'to arise/come into being'. ’n is the contracted form of in; chi = 'whoever' or 'the one who'; la = object pronoun 'it' (referring to the figure); vede = 'sees'. The whole clause means the figure causes genuine bitterness to arise in anyone who sees it, even though the cause is not real.
segments.purgatorio-10-g143: Così = 'thus/so'. Fatti is the past participle 'made/so made' used adjectivally: 'thus made' (i.e., in that form). Vid' is the archaic contracted past 'vidi' (I saw), with the apostrophe showing the dropped vowel; io is the subject 'I'. Color = 'those (people/things)' referring to the earlier figures.
segments.purgatorio-10-g144: Quando = 'when'. Puosi is an archaic past tense (from porre/porre cura → 'porsi cura') meaning here 'I took' or 'I set' in the sense 'I took care / I paid attention'. Ben cura = 'carefully / close attention' (ben modifies cura). The clause means 'when I paid close attention'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g145: Vero è che = 'it is true that' (vero as predicate). Più e meno = 'more and less' (i.e. varying degrees). Eran is the archaic imperfect of essere (were). Contratti is a past participle used adjectivally 'contracted/curled up'. Secondo che/secondo ch’ + avien = 'according as they had' (avien = avevano). Più e meno a dosso = 'more or less on (their) back' (a dosso = on the back or 'on their person'—i.e. how much burden they bore).
segments.purgatorio-10-g146: E qual = 'and whoever' or 'and he who'. Più pazïenza avea = 'had more patience' (avea = imperfect 'had'). Ne li atti = 'in the gestures/acts' (ne = in/during; li = the; atti = acts/gestures). The clause identifies those whose gestures showed more endurance.
segments.purgatorio-10-g147: Piangendo is a gerund 'weeping'. Parea is the imperfect of parere 'seemed' (third person singular); dicer is the archaic infinitive of dire 'to say'. Together piangendo parea dicer = 'while weeping he seemed to say'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g148: Direct speech: 'Più non posso' uses the adverb più with non to mean 'no longer' (lit. 'more not I can'); posso is present tense 'I can'. Word order follows Italian negative with adverb before verb.
segments.purgatorio-10-g53: 'Dinanzi' is an adverb meaning 'ahead' or 'in front' placed at the start for emphasis; 'parea' is the 3rd person singular imperfect of parere, a poetic form meaning 'seemed' (imperfect conveys a continuing or repeated appearance); 'gente' is a collective noun ('people') that functions as the subject/predicate here, so the phrase literally reads 'ahead seemed people'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g54: The coordinating 'e' simply links clauses; 'tutta quanta' is an emphatic expression meaning 'the whole lot' or 'entirely' — 'tutta' (agreeing in form with the feminine collective 'gente') plus 'quanta' used here as an intensifier ('all that' / 'completely').
segments.purgatorio-10-g55: 'Partita' is the past participle used adjectivally from partire in the sense 'divided' or 'separated into parts' and agrees with the collective 'gente' (feminine idea); 'in sette cori' is the prepositional phrase 'into seven choirs' (cori = choirs), describing how the people are grouped.
segments.purgatorio-10-g56: 'A’ due mie’ sensi' = 'to/for my two senses' (the archaic apostrophe indicates elision of the final vowel in 'mie' for the verse); 'faceva dir' is the periphrastic causative (3rd sing. imperfect of fare + infinitive) meaning 'caused to say' or 'made (my senses) say'; 'l’un "No"' = 'the one (of them) "No"' — here 'l’un' = 'l' + uno, 'one (of the two)'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g57: 'L’altro' = 'the other (one)' (again l' = the + altro = other), introducing the second of the two senses; the quoted 'Sì' is the response 'Yes' (a noun/utterance here), beginning the second quoted reply.
segments.purgatorio-10-g58: The single word 'canta’' is the verb cantare in the form of an uttered exhortation 'Sing!' (imperative), written with an apostrophe marking the elision typical of Dante's orthography; it is the content of the quoted reply that the second sense utters.
segments.purgatorio-10-g59: 'Similemente' is an adverb meaning 'similarly' or 'in the same way'; 'al fummo de li ’ncensi' = 'to the smoke of the incense' (Dante uses fummo for 'fumo' = smoke, and 'de li' = of the); the relative clause 'che v’era imaginato' ('that there was imagined') modifies 'fummo'/'ncensi' — 'v’' is the clitic 'vi' meaning 'there' and 'era' is the imperfect of essere ('was'), with 'imaginato' as a past participle describing what had been pictured or imagined there.
segments.purgatorio-10-g60: The clause lists sensory faculties and responses: 'li occhi e ’l naso' ('the eyes and the nose') — two senses in the nominative; 'e al sì e al no' ties in the verbal responses 'the yes and the no' (each introduced by the preposition 'al' = 'to the'); 'discordi' is a plural adjective meaning 'at odds' or 'discordant' agreeing with the compound subject; 'fensi' (from Dante's fense/fensi) means 'feignings' or 'deceptive impressions' — here a plural noun describing the discordant, feigned perceptions.
segments.purgatorio-10-g61: 'Lì' is the locative adverb 'there'; 'precedeva' is the 3rd person singular imperfect of precedere, 'went before' or 'preceded'; 'al benedetto vaso' is the prepositional phrase 'the blessed vessel' (the article + adjective + noun) and denotes what he preceded — word order places the locative first for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-10-g62: 'Trescando' is a present participle/gerund indicating the manner (literally 'trescare' — a musical/trilling motion in Dante's usage) and is used adverbially 'trilling/warbling'; 'alzato' is a past participle meaning 'raised' or 'aloft' and complements the participial phrase (so: 'trilling, raised' or 'with a raised trill').
segments.purgatorio-10-g63: The article 'l’' (elided before a vowel) plus the adjective 'umile' ('humble') and the noun 'salmista' ('psalmist') form the noun phrase 'the humble psalmist' that names the person preceding the vessel.
segments.purgatorio-10-g64: 'E più e men' is an idiom meaning 'more or less' or 'to a greater or lesser degree'; 'che' here functions as the comparative 'than' ('more/less than'); 're' = 'king'; 'era' is the imperfect of essere 'was'; 'in quel caso' = 'in that circumstance/condition' — the whole clause says he was, in that position, more or less like a king.
segments.purgatorio-10-g65: 'Di contra' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'on the other hand' or 'conversely' (di + contra), used to contrast the previous description; it functions as a discourse marker at the start of the clause.
segments.purgatorio-10-g66: 'Effigïata' is a past participle meaning 'depicted' or 'portrayed' (here passive/adjectival); 'ad una vista' means 'to/for a view' or 'as seen at one glance' (ad + una vista); 'd’un gran palazzo' = 'of a great palace' (d' = di + un, gran = grande shortened by verse) — the whole phrase describes how she was pictured, i.e. as the view of a great palace.
segments.purgatorio-10-g67: The proper name 'Micòl' is the subject; 'ammirava' is the imperfect of ammirare ('admired' or 'marvelled at'), here describing a continuing action of admiration; 'sì come' = 'just as' or 'as if'; 'donna dispettosa e trista' is a simile — 'a woman spiteful and sad' — with the two adjectives joined by 'e'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g124: Ed is the conjunction 'and' used to join this speech tag to the previous utterance; elli is an archaic subject pronoun meaning 'he' (the verb 'disse' is omitted, a common poetic ellipsis), and 'a me' is the indirect object 'to me' (the order 'pronoun + a me' places the speaker as recipient of the speech).
segments.purgatorio-10-g125: The noun phrase 'La grave condizione di lor tormento' is the subject ('la' + adjective + noun + genitive of possession 'di lor' = 'their'); 'a terra' is a locative phrase 'to the ground/onto the earth'; the clitic object 'li' (them) precedes the finite verb 'rannicchia' (3rd person singular present 'crouches'), so the verb takes a direct-object clitic rather than a spelled-out object after it.
segments.purgatorio-10-g126: The sequence 'sì che' introduces a result clause meaning 'so that'; 'i miei occhi' ('my eyes') is the subject of the subordinate clause; 'pria' (= 'at first') modifies the verb; 'n’' is the clitic contraction of 'ne' (here 'of it/them'), and 'ebber' is the remote past form of 'avere' used here in the sense 'received/had' (i.e. 'had perception'), while 'tencione' is an archaic/poetic noun meaning attention/perception.
segments.purgatorio-10-g127: Imperative address: 'Ma guarda' = 'But look'; 'fiso' is an adverbial/participial adjective meaning 'fixedly' or 'with a fixed gaze' and follows the verb; 'là' = 'there' specifies where to look.
segments.purgatorio-10-g128: The verb 'disviticchia' is an unusual, figurative verb (literally 'untwines/unentangles') whose subject is understood from context; 'col' is the contraction of 'con il' ('with the'), here 'col viso' literally 'with the face' (i.e. by turning/viewing with his face); 'quel che vien sotto a quei sassi' is a relative clause 'that which comes out from under those stones,' with 'vien' = present 'comes'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g129: 'Già' here is an adverb 'already'/'by now'; the unusual order 'scorger puoi' inverts verb and subject (verb then second-person singular 'puoi'), common in poetry; 'come' introduces a comparison or manner clause 'how/as', and 'ciascun si picchia' means 'each one strikes himself' (reflexive 'si').
segments.purgatorio-10-g130: O is the vocative/exclamatory particle 'Oh'; 'superbi' is an epithet 'proud' modifying the vocative 'cristian' (archaic spelling of 'Christian').
segments.purgatorio-10-g131: Two short vocative/adjectival words 'miseri lassi' (literally 'miserable, weary'), a poetic pair reinforcing blame and pity; both are adjectives in the vocative sense addressing the crowd.
segments.purgatorio-10-g132: A single conjunction 'che' introducing the explanatory relative/causal clause that follows ('who...').
segments.purgatorio-10-g133: A genitive chain 'de la vista de la mente' = 'of the sight of the mind' where 'vista' (sight) is qualified by 'della mente' (of the mind); 'infermi' (sick/infirm) is a predicate adjective describing their state of mind.
segments.purgatorio-10-g134: 'Fidanza avete' is the statement 'you have confidence' (verb after noun), 'ne’' (= 'in') introduces the locative phrase 'ne’ retrosi passi' = 'in the backward steps' (i.e. trusting retrograde measures), with 'retrosi' an adjective modifying 'passi'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g135: Negative interrogative 'non v’accorgete voi che...' = 'do you not notice that...'; 'v’' is the clitic 'vi' (to/for you) attached before the verb, 'accorgete' is the 2nd-person plural present of 'accorgersi' (notice), and the content clause 'noi siam vermi nati a formar l’angelica farfalla' states that 'we are worms born to form the angelic butterfly' (past participle 'nati' with infinitive of purpose 'a formar').
segments.purgatorio-10-g136: Relative clause 'che vola a la giustizia sanza schermi?' where 'che' = 'which', 'vola' = 'flies' (present), 'a la giustizia' = 'to/into justice' (here 'divine justice' or God's judgment), and 'sanza schermi' = 'without shields' (sanza = without). The whole is a rhetorical question reinforcing vulnerability.
segments.purgatorio-10-g137: 'Di che' = 'from/for which' or 'by which' introduces the result; 'l’animo vostro' = 'your spirit/mind' with the possessive 'vostro'; 'in alto' = 'on high' or 'aloft'; 'galla' is an archaic or figurative word here used adverbially/verbally to indicate rising/exulting or a state of galling—its precise meaning is archaic/poetic and must be taken figuratively as 'rejoices/boasts/soars'.
segments.purgatorio-10-g138: 'Poi siete' = 'then you are' (2nd pers. pl. of essere); 'quasi' = 'almost/as if'; 'antomata' is an archaic/rare word here functioning as a predicate adjective (its exact meaning is obscure/archaic — likely 'stunned' or 'deprived of sense'); 'in difetto' = 'in defect' or 'in lack', i.e. 'in a state of deficiency.'
segments.purgatorio-10-g139: Simile introduced by 'sì come' = 'just as/like'; 'vermo' = 'a worm'; 'in cui formazion falla' = 'in whose formation there is a failure' — 'cui' is the relative pronoun referring to 'vermo', 'formazion' = 'formation', and 'falla' = 'a failing/failure' (noun) describing defective formation.
segments.purgatorio-10-g68: I’ mossi i piè del loco dov’ io stava, = I (subject) + past tense verb + object phrase + relative clause. I’ (poetic/io) is the subject, mossi is the first‑person singular passato remoto of muovere (“I moved” / “I set in motion”), i piè is the direct object (“my feet”), del loco is a genitive construction “from/of the place,” and dov’ io stava = dov’ (where) + io + stava (imperfetto of stare, “I was/stood”) giving “from the place where I stood.”
segments.purgatorio-10-g69: per avvisar da presso un’altra istoria, = per + infinitive expresses purpose (“in order to”); avvisar is an infinitive (avvisare, “to inform/bring to notice”), da presso is a locative/adverbial phrase meaning “close by / nearby,” and un’altra istoria = “another tale/narrative.”
segments.purgatorio-10-g70: che di dietro a Micòl mi biancheggiava. = relative clause introduced by che (“which/that”); di dietro a = a compound prepositional phrase meaning “from behind,” Micòl is the proper name, mi is a dative/experiential clitic (“to me”), and biancheggiava is the imperfect of biancheggiare used intransitively to mean “appeared white/gleamed.”
segments.purgatorio-10-g71: Quiv’ era storïata l’alta gloria del roman principato, = Quiv’ (quiv/qui) “here/there,” era (imperfect of essere) functions as copula/auxiliary “was,” storïata is a past participle used adjectivally/passive (“recounted/celebrated”), and the noun phrase l’alta gloria del roman principato is the subject: “the high/lofty glory of the Roman principate.”
segments.purgatorio-10-g72: il cui valore mosse Gregorio a la sua gran vittoria; = relative clause with il cui (“whose”) modifying gloria; valore = “valor/valor/valorous quality,” mosse is a past tense (perfect) meaning “moved” (caused), Gregorio is the agent (Pope Gregory), and a la sua gran vittoria = “to his great victory,” showing result or movement toward that triumph.
segments.purgatorio-10-g73: i’ dico di Traiano imperadore; = i’ (poetic/io) + dico (“I say/I mean”) introduces an explicit identification: “I mean Trajan, emperor.” di before Traiano indicates “of/about/namely” (introducing the person named).
segments.purgatorio-10-g74: e una vedovella li era al freno, = e (“and”) + una vedovella (“a young widow”) + li era al freno: li is a locative/dative clitic here indicating relation to the group/scene (“to him/there”), era = “was,” and al freno = “at the rein/bridle” (a conventional image: she is at the bridle of the leader).
segments.purgatorio-10-g75: di lagrime atteggiata e di dolore. = two descriptive complements introduced by di (“of/with”): di lagrime atteggiata = “made/affected by tears” (atteggiata = posed/affected as if wearing tears) and di dolore = “with sorrow,” both qualifying the widow’s appearance.
segments.purgatorio-10-g76: Intorno a lui parea calcato e pieno di cavalieri, = Intorno a lui “around him”; parea is an impersonal/verb of appearance (imperfetto of parere, “seemed”); calcato (“trodden/pressed”) and pieno (“full”) are predicative adjectives describing the place, followed by di cavalieri (“of knights”), giving “seemed trampled and full of knights.”
segments.purgatorio-10-g77: e l’aguglie ne l’oro sovr’ essi in vista al vento si movieno. = e (“and”) + l’aguglie (plural; here “the little eagles” or eagle‑devices) + ne l’oro (“in the gold,” i.e. on the gold background) + sovr’ essi (“over them/above them”) + in vista (“in sight/visible”) + al vento si movieno (reflexive si + imperfect movere = “moved/they moved” meaning “fluttered/swayed in the wind”).
segments.purgatorio-10-g78: La miserella intra tutti costoro parea dir, = La miserella (“the wretched woman”) + intra tutti costoro (“among all these people”) + parea (imperfetto of parere, “seemed”) + dir (infinitive used after parere, “to say”), i.e. “the poor woman among them seemed to say.”
segments.purgatorio-10-g79: «Segnor, = an address (vocative) using the archaic spelling Segnor (segnor), meaning “Lord” or “Sir.”
segments.purgatorio-10-g80: fammi vendetta di mio figliuol ch’è morto, = fammi (imperative/command form fammi = fa' + mi, “do for me”/“do me”) + vendetta (“vengeance”) + di mio figliuol (“for my son”) + ch’è morto (relative clause “who is dead”); the whole = “do me vengeance for my son who is dead.”
segments.purgatorio-10-g81: ond’ io m’accoro»; = ond’ (ond means “wherefore/from which/therefore”) introducing the consequence or reason + io m’accoro (io + clitic m’ + accoro, present tense “I grieve / I am moved to grief”), i.e. “wherefore I grieve.”
segments.purgatorio-10-g82: ed elli a lei rispondere: = ed (and) + elli (he; archaic/poetic pronoun) + a lei (“to her”) + rispondere (infinitive used to report speech = “answered”), introducing his reply.
segments.purgatorio-10-g83: «Or aspetta tanto ch’i’ torni»; = Or (“now”) + aspetta (imperative “wait”) + tanto (so long, “for so long”) + ch’i’ torni (che + i’ (io) + torni, subjunctive/optative 'that I may return' used temporally = “until I return”).
segments.purgatorio-10-g84: e quella: = e (“and”) + quella (“she/that one”) introducing her speech; simple connective and anaphoric pronoun.
segments.purgatorio-10-g85: «Segnor mio», = a second vocative address, Segnor mio = “my lord,” with possessive mio attached for emphasis/affection.
segments.purgatorio-10-g86: come persona in cui dolor s’affretta, = simile a a phrase “like a person in whom sorrow hastens”: come persona (“like a person”), in cui (“in whom”), dolor (subject) + s’affretta (reflexive form of affrettare in present, “hastens” or “presses forward”), describing her haste of grief that prompts the next question.
segments.purgatorio-10-g87: «se tu non torni?» = direct question introduced by se (“if”), followed by tu (you) + non (not) + torni (present subjunctive/indicative of tornare, “you return” or here simply “you come back”); the sense is conditional/fearful: “if you do not return?”

sentences.purgatorio-10-s1: Then we went within the threshold of the gate that puts away the souls' evil love, for it makes the crooked road appear straight, the sound seeming to be that it was shut again; and if I had turned my eyes toward it, what excuse would have been fitting for the fault?
sentences.purgatorio-10-s2: We crossed over a split stone that moved to and fro, just like a wave that flees and then draws near.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s3: "Here it is fitting to use a little art (skill)," began my duke, "in approaching now on this side, now on that side toward the flank that parts."
sentences.purgatorio-10-s4: And this made our steps few, so that, before the moon's waning had reached her bed to repeat her course, we were outside that narrow opening; but when we were free and open up where the mountain gathers itself behind, I, weary and both of us uncertain of our path, remained on a plain more solitary than roads across deserts.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s25: My eyes, which were content to gaze — to see novelties for which they are eager — were not slow in turning toward him.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s26: I do not, however, want — reader — that you become discouraged in your good resolution on hearing how God wills that the debt be paid.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s27: Do not pay attention to the outward form of the martyrdom: consider the sequence; think that it cannot go beyond the great sentence to anything worse.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s28: I began: "Master, those whom I see moving toward us do not seem to me to be people, and I don't know what they are — I am so bewildered when I look."
sentences.purgatorio-10-s5: From its bank, where the hollow abuts, to the foot of the high cliff that still rises would measure three times a human body; and as far as my eye could stretch with wings — now from the left, now from the right flank — this cornice seemed to me such.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s6: Up there our feet had not yet been moved, when I recognized that surrounding bank which had less of a straight ascent, to be of white marble and so adorned with carvings that not only Polyclitus, but Nature there would have been ashamed.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s7: The angel who came to earth with the decree of the long-lamented peace, who opened heaven from its long prohibition, appeared before us so lifelike, carved there in a gentle gesture, that it did not seem an image that is silent.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s8: One would have sworn that he would say, "Ave!"
sentences.purgatorio-10-s9: '; for there was imagined there that one who, to open lofty Love, turned the key; and she had printed in her gesture the phrase "Ecce ancilla Dei," just as a figure is sealed in wax.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s10: "Do not keep your mind fixed in one place alone," said the sweet master, who had me on that side where the heart is in people.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s11: Wherefore I turned my face, and saw behind Mary, on that side from which he who moved me was, another scene set into the rock; so I passed by Virgil and drew near, that it might be arranged before my eyes.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s12: There, carved into the very marble, was the cart and the oxen drawing the holy Ark; from which it is feared that a duty was not performed.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s21: And he said: "Whoever will be where I am will do it for you"; and she: "What good that belongs to another will be to you, if you put your own into oblivion?"
sentences.purgatorio-10-s22: Whereupon he said: "Now take heart; for it is fitting that I discharge my duty before I move: justice demands it and pity restrains me."
sentences.purgatorio-10-s23: He who never saw anything new produced this visible speech, new to us because it is not found here.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s24: While I delighted in looking at the images of so many humilities, and, because of their Maker, in finding them dear to behold, the poet murmured, 'Look here — but they take short, infrequent steps,' 'many people; these will send some of them to the high steps.'
sentences.purgatorio-10-s33: Just as, to support a floor or a roof, one sometimes sees on a corbel a figure that draws its knees up to its chest, which makes a real bitterness arise from what is not true in whoever sees it; so I saw those figures, when I took careful notice.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s34: It is true that they were more or less contracted, according as they had more or less on their backs; and whoever had greater patience in his gestures, while weeping seemed to say: 'I can no longer.'
sentences.purgatorio-10-s13: Ahead there seemed to be people; and all of them, divided into seven choirs, made my two senses utter one ‘No’, the other ‘Yes — sing’.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s14: Similarly to the smoke of the incense that had been imagined there, the eyes and the nose, and the 'yes' and the 'no', were at odds as deceptive impressions.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s15: There, ahead of the blessed vessel went, trilling in a raised voice, the humble psalmist, and in that condition he was more or less like a king.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s16: On the other hand, depicted as in the view of a great palace, Micòl admired it — like a spiteful and sad woman.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s29: And he to me: “The heavy state of their torment crouches them to the ground, so that at first my eyes had no perception (of them).”
sentences.purgatorio-10-s30: But look fixedly there, and with your face disentangle/turn aside what comes from under those rocks: already you can discern how each one strikes himself.”
sentences.purgatorio-10-s31: O proud Christians, miserable and weary ones, who, sick in the sight of the mind, put trust in backward steps, do you not realize that we are worms born to make the angelic butterfly, which flies to divine justice without shields?
sentences.purgatorio-10-s32: From this your spirit soars high, then you are almost stunned by defect, just like a worm in whose making there is a fault?
sentences.purgatorio-10-s17: I moved my feet from the place where I stood, to bring nearer another tale that was gleaming behind Micòl to me.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s18: Here the lofty glory of the Roman principate was recounted, whose valor moved Gregory to his great victory; I mean Emperor Trajan; and a young widow was at his rein, made up of tears and sorrow.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s19: Around him it seemed trampled and full of knights, and the eagles worked into the gold above them moved in sight in the wind.
sentences.purgatorio-10-s20: The poor creature among all these seemed to say: “Lord, do me vengeance for my son who is dead, whereof I grieve”; and he to her replied: “Now wait until I return”; and she: “My lord,” like a person in whom sorrow hastens, “what if you do not return?”
